coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield as an effective fluorescence and magnetic resonance bimodal imaging probe
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Caiyan
Xuan, Tongtong
Chen, Yiwei
Zhao, Zhenjie
Liu, Xiaoxiao
Lian, Guohai
Li, Huili
coredata.dc:description: It is highly desired to develop the dual-modality fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) probes in medical imaging because it can provide high-resolution macroscopical anatomical information and high-sensitivity microscopical optical signal simultaneously. In this study, harmless gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were prepared via a convenient one-pot hydrothermal approach for fluorescence/MR bimodal imaging. The derived Gd-CDs exhibit enhanced blue photoluminescence with a quantum yield as high as 69.86% and significantly improved longitudinal relaxivity (r 1 = 14.33 mM−1 s−1, 0.5 T) in comparison with commercial Magnevist (Gd-DTPA, r 1 = 4.5 mM−1 s−1, 0.5 T). Here Gd3+ ions are simply chelated onto CDs by carboxyl groups. Moreover, unlike the previous reports, Gd3+ chelation does not perturb core optical properties of CDs. Excellent water solubility, good cell-membrane permeability and negligible cytotoxicity make Gd-CDs an ideal dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobe, suggesting its potential and significance in practical biological and clinic applications in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots
Hydrothermal approach
Quantum yield
Longitudinal relaxivity
Fluorescence/magnetic resonance bimodal imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Gd-CDs prepared at 180 °C for 3 h. Inset shows HRTEM image of single nanoparticle. (b) Size distribution histogram of Gd-CDs dispersed in distilled water. (c) EDS chemical composition of Gd-CDs on a TEM copper grid.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman, and (c) FTIR spectra of the Gd-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) Gd 4d spectrum, (c) XPS magnification and fitting curves of C 1 s spectrum, (d) O 1 s spectrum, and (e) N 1 s spectrum for the prepared Gd-CDs.

The UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra (λex = 360 nm) of the Gd-CDs doped with different Gd3+ concentration, and the lower right inset shows photographs taken under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).
The UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra (λex = 360 nm) of the Gd-CDs (a) with various DETA concentrations prepared (b) at 150–200 °C (c) for 0.5–5 h.

Cell toxicity and cell imaging of Gd-CDs: (a) effect of Gd-CDs on HeLa cells viability, (b) bright-field microphotograph, (c) confocal fluorescence microphotograph, and (d) overlap of corresponding bright-field image and fluorescence image of HeLa cells incubated with the Gd-CDs in a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 for 2 h. Cells were imaged using a 63 × oil-immersion objective.
(a) Linear relationship between r 1 (T 1 −1) and Gd concentrations and (b) T1-weighted MR images of Gd-CDs different Gd concentrations. The value of T 1 −1 was collected basing on readings from (b).
Schematic illustration for the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of Gd-CDs as a bimodal imaging probe.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield as an effective fluorescence and magnetic resonance bimodal imaging probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advanced Instrument

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Electric Power Research Institute of State Grid, Changsha 410007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Electric Power Research Institute of State Grid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marketing Department, Hunan Electric Power Company of State Grid, Changsha 410007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Electric Power Company of State Grid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is highly desired to develop the dual-modality fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) probes in medical imaging because it can provide high-resolution macroscopical anatomical information and high-sensitivity microscopical optical signal simultaneously. In this study, harmless gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were prepared via a convenient one-pot hydrothermal approach for fluorescence/MR bimodal imaging. The derived Gd-CDs exhibit enhanced blue photoluminescence with a quantum yield as high as 69.86% and significantly improved longitudinal relaxivity (r 1 = 14.33 mM−1 s−1, 0.5 T) in comparison with commercial Magnevist (Gd-DTPA, r 1 = 4.5 mM−1 s−1, 0.5 T). Here Gd3+ ions are simply chelated onto CDs by carboxyl groups. Moreover, unlike the previous reports, Gd3+ chelation does not perturb core optical properties of CDs. Excellent water solubility, good cell-membrane permeability and negligible cytotoxicity make Gd-CDs an ideal dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobe, suggesting its potential and significance in practical biological and clinic applications in the future.

Gd-CDs with maximum quantum yield of 69.86% and improved longitudinal relaxivity of 14.33 mM−1 s−1 were synthesized via a convenient one-step hydrothermal method. Excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, and negligible cytotoxicity make Gd-CDs an ideal dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging nanoprobe in practical biological and clinic applications in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is regarded as one of most powerful techniques in modern diagnostic medicine because it can penetrate deeply into tissue, providing anatomical details and high quality three-dimensional images of soft tissue in a non-invasive monitoring manner [1,2]. Unfortunately, this technique has just been able to resolve objects larger than a few micrometers in size, thus exhibits much lower sensitivity than radioactive or optical method [3,4]. Fluorescence imaging (FI), in contrast, has the capacity for single-cell sensitivity and subcellular resolution, but possesses poor spatial resolution and tissue penetration, which restricts its application just to surface or near-surface phenomena [5–7]. Apparently, combining the advantages of MRI and FI can bridge gaps in sensitivity and depth between these two modalities, and consequently, leads to an improved reliability in diagnosis [8–10].

Up to now, developing FI/MRI bimodal nanoprobes usually adopt two different strategies. One logical strategy is based on incorporating paramagnetic ions into semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) such as CdSe, CdS and CdTe [10–13]. These nanoprobes show good photostability, high relaxivity and quantum yield (QY), but high toxicity and potential pollution hazard limit their biological and medical applications [10–13]. The other is to coat the magnetic cores and fluorophores with shells such as silica or modifying silica-capped fluorescent cores with paramagnetic ions [14,15]. In general, these probes have lower toxicity compared with those obtained by the above strategy thanks to the encapsulation of silica shell. However, silica encapsulating not only makes the synthesis process more complex and difficult, but also increases the particle size by a value of 4–7 nm [14,15].

Current trend in the development of dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probes suggests that an ideal nanoprobe should not only own stronger fluorescent intensity and higher relaxivity, but also should possess low toxicity and good stability in a biological environment. Gadolinium (Gd) has been confirmed to exhibit excellent contrast efficiency because of its unique magnetic property. Many Gd-containing nanoparticles and chelates have been developed as effective probes for MR diagnosis [16–21]. What's more, some Gd chelates are commercially available, but they are expensive and their contrast efficiency needs to be further improved. Therefore, one challenge for MRI contrast agents is to design Gd-containing agents with high contrast efficiency, high stability, low-cost, low toxicity, and easy preparation process [22,23]. As a rising star of the carbon family, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is becoming a superior framework to construct multi-modal imaging probes, because of their favorable optical properties, good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, and low toxicity along with green synthesis and the ease of surface functionalization [24,25]. Bourlinos [4] and Ren [26] as well as their co-workers reported the synthesis of Gd-doped CDs for fluorescence/MR imaging by the low temperature pyrolysis of the precursors containing Gd and carbon. The resultant Gd-doped CDs showed good MR response and photoluminescence (PL) properties with longitudinal relaxation rate (r 1) and QY of 5.5–6.4 mM−1 s−1 and 2.6–8.9%, respectively [4,26]. One-pot approach such as hydrothermal treatment of citrate acid (CA), ethanediamine and GdCl3 at 200 °C [8], or microwave-assisted polyol method using sucrose, concentrated H2SO4 and GdCl3 as the starting materials [22] had also been successfully demonstrated to prepare Gd-doped CDs with a largely improved r 1 of 11.356 mM−1 s−1, but a low QY of 5.4% was accompanied. Additionally, Gd3+ encapsulated carbonaceous dots were also prepared by simple calcination of gadopentetic acid (Gd-DTPA) in the air [27]. Although a relatively high QY of 19.7% was present, the r 1 value of 5.88 mM−1 s−1 was unsatisfactory [27]. Recently, Shi et al. reported a dual-modal imaging probe based on Gd chelates functionalized CDs via a complex three-step approach [28]. r 1 of Gd-doped CDs was sharply improved to 56.72 mM−1 s−1 due to increasing their surface-chelated Gd3+. However, compared with Chen's work [27], the QY was degenerated to 5.4%, suggesting that an enhancement of MR property is at the expense of sacrificing fluorescent intensity. Also, the involving three-step process is cost- and time-consuming [28]. Therefore, the development of multimodal imaging nanoprobes based on CDs still remains in the early stage. Considering the diversity in the preparation, structure, surface functionalization, and PL property of CDs, continued efforts should be exerted.

It is well known that free Gd3+ is highly toxic because they inhibit calcium channels and cause cardiovascular and neurologic toxicity [20,29]. Therefore, decreasing total amount of Gd3+ in Gd-doped CDs while maintaining the excellent MR and fluorescent properties, would favor their practical applications in bio-imaging. In this study, a dual-modal imaging probe with high fluorescence and improved relaxivity for T 1-weighted MR imaging was prepared and investigated in detail. As shown in Scheme 1 , Gd-doped CDs were synthesized via a convenient one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), and gadolinium chloride (GdCl3) as the starting materials. CA was selected as carbon source because it can provide more carboxyl groups which may chelate gadolinium ions during and after formation of CDs than other nontoxic acid [8,22,29]. Moreover, CA is more easily polymerized in the presence of DETA [30,31]. As-prepared Gd-doped CDs exhibits excellent water solubility and cell-membrane permeability, which provides potentials for dual-modal MR/fluorescence imaging in vitro and in vivo. Experimental results indicate that the synthesized Gd-CDs in the present work contain lower Gd3+ content, while exhibiting good longitudinal relaxivity, significantly improved QY, and negligible cytotoxicity. Most importantly, the doping of paramagnetic Gd3+ does not cause the loss of fluorescent intensity but largely enhances the QY of CDs to 69.86%. Gd-doped CDs have also been demonstrated for dual-modal MR/fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells in real-time, suggesting that these nanoprobes have great potential applications in biological and medical fields in the future.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, CA) and diethylenetriamine (C4H13N3, DETA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis membranes of 500–1000 Da (USA, Spectrumlabs) were purchased from Toscience Biotechnology Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8), gadolinium chloride (GdCl3), Xylenol orange (XO) and Eriochrome black T (EBT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Trading Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were obtained from Gibco Life Technologies Co. (Grand Island, USA). Human cervical carcinoma (HeLa cells) was purchased from Shanghai Institute of Cell Biology (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Distilled water was used throughout the whole experiment.

CDs and Gd-CDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal treatment of CA and GdCl3 in the presence of DETA. Briefly, 3 g CA, 0–1.2348 g GdCl3 (0–5 at.%), and 0–1 mL DETA were dispersed into 6 mL distilled water in sequence under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the above mixture solution was added into a 10 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 150–200 °C for 0.5–5 h. The dark brown products were obtained after cooling to room temperature. The large and agglomerated nanoparticles were removed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The resultant supernatant containing Gd-CDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-porous membrane to further remove residual large particles. Final purification of the Gd-CDs solution was conducted through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 48 h (changed deionized water every 4 h). After this process, a light brown Gd-CDs aqueous dispersion was obtained for further characterization and application.

The phase evolution was characterized by a M21XVHF2Z (Mac Science Co. Ltd.) X-ray diffractometer (XRD), using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å) at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA with 2θ scanning mode. The morphology and particle size of Gd-CDs were observed by a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, Japan) at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The element compositions were measured by an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) on the TEM. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman Spectrometer (RM-1000, Renishaw) with 632.8 nm He-Ne laser as an irradiation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by a Multifunction Imaging Photoelectron Spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) in the region from 4000 to 500 cm−1 were recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 600 FTIR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). The absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared Gd-CDs were measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4), respectively. The QY of the Gd-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q
Y x
=
QY
R
I x
I R
A R
A x
n x 2
n R 2
where I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, which is measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The subscript “x” and “R” designate Gd-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as a standard sample. QY x = 54% at 360 nm and n R = 1.33 [32–34]. The Gd-CDs were dissolved in distilled water with n x = 1.33. In order to minimize the effect of reabsorption, the absorbance values of all samples at the excitation wavelength were kept below 0.10.

Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of as-prepared Gd-CDs on HeLa cells (human cervical carcinoma cells) according to the following procedure. Briefly, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 1 × 104 cells per well in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. After incubating the cells for 24 h, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing the different concentration of Gd-CDs (from 0 mg mL−1 to 2 mg mL−1). At certain times (24 h or 48 h), the medium was removed, and fresh medium (100 μL) containing CCK-8 (20 μL, 5 mg mL−1) was added into each well, and then the cells were incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. The relative viability of cells was assessed by measuring the absorbance of the solution at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo).

HeLa cells (1 × 104) were plated in a cover-glass-bottom dish in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. After culturing overnight in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37 °C, the culture medium was replaced with fresh DMEM containing Gd-CDs of 0.5 mg mL−1. And then, the cells were further incubated for 2 h and rinsed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for three times to remove the excess Gd-CDs. Finally, the images were taken by a confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss LSM 700) excitated at 405 nm.

The T1-weighted relaxivity of Gd-CDs was obtained on a 0.5 T MRI instrument (MesoMR60, Shanghai Niumag Corporation, China). The samples were dispersed in double-distilled water with various Gd3+ concentrations (0, 0.3, 0.6, 1.12, 1.41 and 2.82 mM). Relaxivity values of Gd-CDs were obtained from the slope of the linear fitting of 1/T relaxation time (s−1) versus Gd3+ concentration (mM). The T1-weighted images were acquired using the sequence of RARE-T1 + T2-map under the following parameters: TE = 12.5 ms, TR = 100 ms, field of view (FOV) = 10 × 10 cm2, flip angle (FA) = 180°, and slice thickness = 4 mm. Matrix = 192 × 256 cm.

The morphology, particle size distribution, and EDS chemical composition of Gd-CDs fabricated by the hydrothermal method are present in Fig. 1 . It can be seen that the obtained Gd-CDs are well-separated each other (Fig. 1a) and exhibit a nearly spherical shape with the size distribution between 2.6 and 6.5 nm (Fig. 1b). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis shows a single narrow peak at ∼ 5.5 nm (Fig. S1), which is well correlated with the TEM result and also suggests that the as-synthesized Gd-CDs are highly dispersible in aqueous solution. It maintains colloidal stability for several months in solution without visible precipitation and at least 48 h in 0.01 M PBS and serum (Fig. S2). The HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1a) reveals low diffraction contrast and very faint lattice fringe, indicating the poor crystalline of the carbon. EDS analysis confirms the existence of Gd in the hybrid material (Fig. 1c).

The XRD profile depicted in Fig. 2 a consists of a weak broad diffraction peak at around 23.9° corresponding to an interlayer spacing (d) of 0.41 nm, which is larger than that of bulk graphite (0.34 nm) [24,25,35]. The oxygen-containing groups introduced in the synthesis process may be in charge of the interplanar distance enhancing [24,25,35–37]. Additionally, the increase in d value represents that the phase tends to be amorphous nature, which further verifies the poor crystallinity of as-prepared Gd-CDs and is consistent with the HRTEM result in Fig. 1a. Raman spectrum (Fig. 2b) consists of two broad peaks at around 1361 (D-band) and 1577 cm−1 (G-band), corresponding to the sp 3 and sp 2 hybrid carbons, respectively. The D-band originates from a hybridized vibrational mode (A 1g) associated with graphene edges and indicates the presence of some disordered graphene structure [38,39]. The G band corresponds to the in-plane vibration mode E 2g of the graphite [37–39]. The co-existence of the two bands suggests partial disordered graphite-like structure of the Gd-CDs. The result is in concert with TEM and XRD again. FTIR spectrum was used to identify the functional groups presented on the surface of Gd-CDs (Fig. 2c). The appearance of a couple of absorption bands is attributed to the oxygen-containing groups. For instance, the peak at 1033 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of COC and CO bonds [36,40]; the characteristic absorption band at 1149 cm−1 results from the stretching modes of CO and CN [39]; the peaks between 1496 and 1398 cm−1 can be identified as CN, NH, and COO− groups [34,38,39]; the band around 1650 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CO and CC elementary units; a small band at 2938 cm−1 comes from the CH bond, and the broad band at 3246-3495 cm−1 is derived from the stretching modes of OH and NH [36,42]. It has been demonstrated that these oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups, on the edge of the carbon backbone are the common origin for the fluorescence in the CDs [43,44]. In addition, the presence of CN and NH bonds suggests that Gd-CDs have been functionalized by DETA, which can be further demonstrated in the following XPS data.

The chemical composition of Gd-CDs was further probed by XPS technique. The binding energy was calibrated with C 1 s at 284.8 eV as a reference. The characteristic peaks corresponding to Gd 4d (142.3 eV and 147.2 eV), C 1 s (284.8 eV and 288.8 eV), N 1s (399.6 eV) and O 1 s (532.5 eV) are observed in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a), confirming the presence of Gd in the product with 71.33 at.% C, 23.65 at.% O, 1.54 at.% Gd and 3.48 at.% N. No Cl ions are detected by XPS analysis, indicating that unreacted GdCl3 was removed completely. The high-resolution XPS Gd 4d spectrum exhibits two lines at 142.3 and 147.2 eV (Fig. 3b), respectively, suggesting that the oxidation state of Gd remains +3 in Gd-CDs. Note that the position of these two lines is higher in comparison to the corresponding 4d signals (141.2 eV) of Gd3+ in Gd2O3, which is probably due to the different coordination environments [8,45]. This, in turn, strongly validates the Gd successful chelation onto the surface of CDs. In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c), four peaks are observed with the binding energies of about 284.6, 285.2, 285.9, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO (epoxy COC and alkoxy COH), and CO species, respectively [37,44]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d) consists of two kinds of oxygen bonds for CO at 532.2 eV and CO at 533.3 eV [37,38]. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3e has two peaks at 399.5 and 400.2 eV, which are attributed to the pyridinic N (=NH-) and pyrrolic N (NH), respectively [39,47]. All these results demonstrate once more that the synthesized carbon nanoparticles containing Gd3+ are functionalized by hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties which originate from citric acid [36–41]. The presence of these functional groups imparts excellent solubility in water of CDs [48,49]. Additionally, the appearance of N 1 s peak further indicates the role of DETA, and N-doping maybe enhances the fluorescent property of Gd-CDs.

Typical UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of un-doped and Gd-containing CDs synthesized at 180 °C for 3 h are shown in Fig. 4 . It is observed that the absorption and PL peak positions of both CDs and Gd-CDs synthesized under the same condition resemble completely each other, implying that the incorporation of Gd3+ ions does not obviously influence on the particle size distribution of CDs and the core optical properties of CDs are not perturbed. One absorption peak at 245 nm originates from the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC units within the graphene structure [22,47,50]. The other band at 356 nm should be ascribed to n-π* transition of CO bands, indicating the presence of carboxyl/carbonyl groups on the surface of Gd-CDs [6,30,33]. Upon a UV light (360 nm) excitation, both un-doped and Gd-doped CDs emit a bright blue fluorescence centered at 448 nm. Although there is no apparent wavelength shift between as-prepared CDs and Gd-CDs in their excitation and emission spectra, PL intensity is enhanced dramatically after Gd3+ is coordinated to CDs, and reaches a maximum at 2 at.% Gd3+ concentration. This variation is completely different from the previous studies in which Gd3+ decreased fluorescence efficiency of CDs [28]. A possible explanation for the increase in PL is that the doped Gd disorders the carbon rings and produces new emission energy traps [26,27]. Furthermore, the PL spectra of CDs and Gd-CDs with progressively increased excitation wavelengths are recorded and given in Fig. S3. When the excitation wavelength is varied from 320 to 420 nm, the PL intensity of blue emission reaches the maximum at 360 nm excitation regardless of with or without Gd. However, the fluorescence emission peak at 448 nm remains unaltered. Such excitation-independent PL behavior has also been reported by other groups [42,46]. It is considered to be related to less surface defects and more uniform size distribution of CDs, which matches well with TEM observation. The wavelength-independent phenomenon makes the Gd-doped CDs useful for FI where the unwanted auto-fluorescence (e.g. tissue auto-fluorescence) can be avoided [51,52].

It is well-known that carbonization and passivation at low temperature would be beneficial to obtain well functionalized CDs. Herein, we choose CA as the only carbon source due to its low carbonization temperature of <200 °C [8,37]. In addition, more carboxyl groups from CA may chelate gadolinium ions during and after formation of CDs. DETA is selected as a functionalizing reagent since it can provide the amine groups to passivate CDs and incorporate N into the carbon framework, which has been confirmed by FTIR and XPS spectra in Figs. 2c and 3e. It has been proven that N-doping can modify the electron density, improve the chemical activities and photic properties, and finally increase PL intensity of the host material [6,53,54]. Therefore, in order to obtain Gd-CDs with highest fluorescence intensity, the synthesis conditions, such as the hydrothermal temperature, reaction time and the concentration of DETA, were optimized and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Obviously, the absorption intensity continuously increases with the hydrothermal reaction temperature, time and the concentration of DETA increasing. However, the emission spectra contain a maximum in intensity at 180 °C for 3 h with a DETA concentration of 0.4646 M for CDs doped with 2 at.% Gd3+. That is to say, Gd-CDs synthesized at this condition own the highest QY of 69.86% when using quinine sulfate as the reference (Fig. S4), which is much higher than those of previous studies (2.6%–21%) [22,26–28,31]. Therefore, Gd-CDs synthesized in the present work is completely comparable to and even better than most of CDs-based dual modal imaging agents only from the point of fluorescence-guided surgery. Such high fluorescence QY may be related to simultaneously introducing Gd and N into the carbon framework [24,25,37–39].

Gd3+ can induce serious cytotoxicity by disrupting critical Ca2+ signaling in cells [53]. Previous reports have proved that IC50 and LD50 for free Gd3+ is ∼0.2 mM kg−1 and 0.4 mM kg−1, respectively [29]. So, to minimize the toxicity of CDs themselves, citric acid, a nontoxic organic polyprotic acid, was used as carbon source in this paper. The inherent cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs was evaluated using HeLa cells through CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 6 a, cell viability is above 90% even when Gd-CDs concentration reaches 1 mg mL−1 (equivalent Gd3+ concentration is 1 mM) and incubation time prolongs 48 h, almost same as that of pure CDs (Fig. S5). It confirms good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs towards HeLa cells, not like free gadolinium ions. The strong Gd3+-carboxyl chelation is expected to effectively block the leakage of Gd3+ into the surroundings [31]. To verify this hypothesis, Gd-CDs with different concentrations were stabilized in distilled water with 2–11 pH values for 48 h Gd3+ releasing into the surroundings was collected through dialysis and detected using Xylenol Orange (XO) and Eriochrome Black T (EBT) as the indicator, respectively, because XO is sensitive at 2–6 pH, while EBT is sensitive at 7–11. As shown in Fig. S6, both XO and EBT solution can react with free gadolinium ions (GdCl3) to form red substance even at free Gd3+ concentration as low as 0.125 mM and pH increasing from 2 to 11. On the contrary, the Gd-CDs could not react with them to form red substance at the investigated concentration as high as 2 mg mL−1 (equal to 2 mM Gd3+ content). It indicates that the binding between the Gd3+ and CDs is stronger than that between the Gd3+ and XO or EBT. No leakage of Gd3+ ions or Gd3+ concentration from leakage of Gd-CDs is far less than 0.125 mM even when they were stabilized in distilled water for 48 h under various pH from 2 to 11, which is safe enough in practical biological applications comparable with the standard of IC50 ≈ 0.2 mM for free Gd3+ [29].

The potential application of as-prepared Gd-CDs as fluorescent probes for FI was explored. Fig. 6b–d presents the fluorescence images of living HeLa cells incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 Gd-CDs aqueous solution for 2 h. No fluorescence is observed for the control bright-field image (Fig. 6b). On the contrary, the phase contrast image of HeLa cells at λex = 405 nm, as depicted in Fig. 6c, clearly shows the strong blue fluorescence, but, no auto-fluorescence from the cells incubated without Gd-CDs is observed (Fig. S7). Thus, it can be concluded that Gd-CDs permeate inside the cells by a possible endocytosis mechanism [6,24,55,56]. From the overplayed fluorescence and bright-field images (Fig. 6d), it can be seen in further that the PL signals are from the perinuclear regions of the cytosol, and also no morphological damage of the HeLa cells appear after being labelled with Gd-CDs, which suggests that the cells are live as before. Besides, there is no reduction in luminescence intensity even after excitation for a prolonged time. All these results firmly demonstrate that the as-synthesized Gd-CDs by using a nontoxic citric acid in this work possess good biocompatibility and can serve as a potential candidate for organic dyes or semiconductor QDs in fluorescence cell imaging.

After Gd3+ doping, the unique PL properties of CDs were well preserved and the QY was enhanced. More importantly, the introduction of Gd3+ renders CDs the MRI modality because it can accelerate longitudinal (T1) relaxation of water protons and exert bright contrast in regions where the nanoprobes localize. Thus, we investigate the T1 contrast capability of Gd-CDs as MRI contrast agents on a 0.5 T MRI instrument and the results are present in Fig. 7 . Obviously, longitudinal relaxivity of Gd-CDs is enhanced linearly along with the increase of equivalent Gd concentration (Fig. 7a). The specific relaxivity values (r1) evaluated from a plot of the relaxation time (T1 −1) versus the Gd3+ ion concentration is determined to be 14.33 mM−1 s−1, which is much larger than commercially available MRI agent Magnevist (r1 = 4.5 mM−1 s−1, 0.5 T) [9] and recently reported Gd-doped CDs (Table S1, r1 = 5.88–11.36 mM−1 s−1) [4,8,22,26–28]. Especially noted, although CDs-DTPA-Gd with r1 as high as 56.72 mM−1 s−1 has been synthesized and investigated by Shi and his/her co-workers [28], the QY of only 5.4% is far less than the value of 69.86% in this work. In the case of a nanoparticle based MRI contrast agent, small size is important to reach a high longitudinal relaxivity because longitudinal relaxivity is largely dependent upon direct interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons [22,57,58]. The ultra-small Gd-CDs with high specific surface ratios could maximize dipole-dipole interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons and, thus, are responsible for the high r1. Consistently, the gray-scaled T1-weighted images of Gd-CDs with different Gd3+ concentrations clearly show a positive contrast enhancement, and the brightness become more prominent as increasing Gd3+ concentration from 0 to 2.8 mM (Fig. 7b). But, no such signal enhancement is observed for bare CDs. Therefore, we can conclude that Gd-CDs synthesized in this study hold greater potential as fluorescence/MR bimodal nanoprobe in medical imaging for a better diagnosis.

In summary, a high quality bimodal bio-imaging nanoprobe based on Gd-CDs was synthesized though a simple one-pot hydrothermal method by using nontoxic citric acid as carbon source. While CDs passivated Gd3+ ions to decrease their leakage and toxicity, the fluorescence of CDs was significantly enhanced. QY for Gd-CDs reaches as high as 69.86%. The strong coordination between the Gd3+ and CDs led to a stable structure of Gd-CDs which causes an enhanced MRI contrast with an r1 value as high as 14.33 mM−1 s−1. The CKK-8 assay disclosed that the Gd-CDs had a minor impact on the HeLa cells viability. In vitro relaxivity characterization and cell imaging demonstrate that Gd-CDs possess not only the high sensitivity of fluorescence imaging but also the high spatial resolution of MRI, providing a great potential for its practical applications in biological and clinical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51472087), Shanghai Municipal Natural Science Foundation (No.13ZR1412500), Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No.14ZZ050), and the ECNU Reward for Out-standing Doctoral Dissertation Cultivation Plan of Action (No. PY2015041).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2016.07.226.
